videos.title: Music Isn't Chopped Liver
videos.description:
Think your technique is solid? Prove it—play a blues in Ab.

If that throws you off, the issue isn’t your chops… it’s your connection to music. Technique only matters if it transfers into real playing.

Music is what builds that bridge. It sharpens your hands, your timing, your instincts—and ultimately opens doors.

Don’t follow anyone who tells you otherwise.

videos.title: Dani Rabin Comes Clean. Me, too!
videos.description:
Outspoken fusion guitarist, dani rabin shares his thought with jeff in an open and honest conversation about music education and the dedication to music itself.

Topics Covered in This Episode
 • Speaking Truth in Music (and Online)
The risks and rewards of sharing honest opinions in today’s highly reactive culture.
 • “Dissonance Signaling” in Jazz Culture
How musicians drift away from authentic taste to chase trends and validation.
 • The Problem with Modern Music Education
A blunt critique of institutions like Berklee and whether they truly prepare students for real careers.
 • Student Debt vs. Real-World Musicianship
Why expensive music degrees can limit — rather than launch — a working musician’s life.
 • Bass Education & “No Wrong Notes” Philosophy
A deep dive into how vague teaching methods may be hurting developing players.
 • Playing vs. Practicing: Two Different Worlds
Why real growth happens on gigs — not just in the practice room.
 • Improvisation: Playing “Connected” vs. Thinking
The importance of staying in the musical moment instead of relying on theory mid-performance.
 • Why Most Great Musicians Master One Style
Debunking the idea that you need to learn everything to succeed.
 • Handling Criticism, Hate, and Internet Culture
Dani’s rules: don’t be offended, be funny, and never apologize.
 • The Reality of Building a Music Career Today
Writing, touring, teaching, and staying authentic in a noisy digital world.
 • Metronomes, Time Feel & Physical Motion
A nuanced take on how tempo relates to body movement and internal timing.

#DaniRabin #JeffBerlin #MusicTruth #BassEducation #JazzEducation #MusicInterview #NothingButTheTruth #MusicPhilosophy

videos.title: Why Bass Teachers Won’t Say This Publicly.
videos.description:
For years, people have said that no respected teachers agree with Jeff Berlin’s criticisms of bass education.

But privately?
Some do.

Why don’t they say it publicly?

Because the internet can be vicious.
Because businesses depend on popularity.
Because the bass community often prioritizes performance over learning.

Jeff makes a distinction many ignore:
Time is learned.
Groove is performed.

Most great bass players found groove after learning how to play music properly.

When “groove first” became the focus, musical development often stopped.

Jeff’s position hasn’t changed:
Music comes first.
Notes come first.
Learning time comes first.

Publicly or privately, that principle remains.

Your thoughts are welcome.

#JeffBerlin #BassEducation #MusicTheory #TimeAndGroove #BassGuitar #MusicTeaching

videos.title: Would Bass Players Be Better Without the Industry?
videos.description:
Jeff Berlin poses a thought experiment:

What if every bass class, online lesson, music college program, camp, and celebrity-endorsed teaching platform disappeared tomorrow?

Would bass players suffer?
Or would only the serious musicians remain?

If the fantasy of instant success vanished…
if industry marketing stopped…
if popularity and style were no longer the focus…

What would be left?

Jeff believes something simple would remain:
Self-motivated musicians.
Academic music.
Written harmony.
Real learning.

The pursuit of music has always been self-driven.
No one solicits people into opera.
Why should bass be different?

If everything else disappeared, would it actually hurt the instrument — or clarify it?

Your thoughts are welcome.

#JeffBerlin #BassEducation #MusicTheory #BassGuitar #LearnMusic #MusicPhilosophy

videos.title: The Reason I’m Exiled from Bass Education.
videos.description:
Jeff Berlin explains why he isn’t welcome at most bass education gatherings.

For decades, he has argued that bass players cannot improve without real musical content — harmony, notes, written music — not community reinforcement or philosophical encouragement.

If your goal is to play for fun with friends, none of this criticism applies.

But if your goal is real improvement, the logic is simple:
You need music to improve on a musical instrument.

Jeff believes bass education drifted away from music itself and toward personality, community, and emotional reassurance.

The good news?
One year of practicing proper musical content can undo twenty years of confusion.

Your thoughts are welcome.

#JeffBerlin #BassEducation #MusicTheory #BassGuitar #LearnMusic #MusicTeaching

videos.title: Rhythm Isn't Enough
videos.description:
In this episode of And Nothing But The Truth, Jeff responds to a common question: “Why are you always critical of bass education and why not show something positive?”

Jeff breaks down a popular online “subdivision/accent” exercise and explains why, even if it feels productive, it can turn players into a temporary “one-note in 4/4” solution that doesn’t translate to real gigs, real music-making, or better musicianship. The point isn’t to insult teachers or students, it’s to challenge a flawed product: practice methods that don’t actually improve the player.

Drawing from his classical training and decades of real-world experience, Jeff argues for learning differently by putting music first, building usable skills, and focusing on what actually makes you a better musician. As he quotes Gloria Steinem: “The truth will set you free, but first it will piss you off.”

Like, subscribe, and hit the alert button for more episodes of And Nothing But The Truth.

videos.title: Everyone Learns the Same Way. Yes, Everyone.
videos.description:
After teaching many recent lessons, Jeff Berlin noticed something striking:

Every student shares the same core issue — a lack of harmonic understanding.

It doesn’t matter the style.
It doesn’t matter the level.
The principle is identical.

Despite the common belief that “everyone learns differently,” Jeff argues that music learning follows one proven path: understanding harmony through the mechanical regard of notes.

Read it.
Write it.
Practice it.
Internalize it.

What’s often missing isn’t talent — it’s proper musical content.

And once harmony becomes the foundation, bass education becomes clear instead of vague.

Mechanical understanding.
Artistic expression.
Both matter — but in the right order.

Your thoughts are welcome.

#JeffBerlin #BassEducation #MusicTheory #LearnHarmony #BassGuitar #MusicTeaching

videos.title: You can't be prepared for a career by a music school | The Truth About Bass Education
videos.description:
Are music schools really preparing bass players for careers… or selling a promise that never existed?

In this episode of And Nothing But The Truth, I break down the philosophy behind modern bass education and compare it to the original intent of the Schillinger House (now Berklee College of Music). 

Back in 1945, students weren’t promised careers — they were taught music. The assumption was simple: become qualified, and opportunity becomes possible.

Today, the message has shifted. Many students are told that if they follow a certain curriculum, a career is imminent. History shows that was never the guarantee.

Most musicians already have experience before entering music school — playing in bands, gigging regionally, building real-world skills. So what should education actually focus on?

The answer is simple:
Put music first.
Master the instrument.
Qualification precedes opportunity.

Careers are a byproduct — not a syllabus outcome.

If you're investing time, money, and hope into music education, this conversation matters.

Subscribe for more episodes of And Nothing But The Truth.

videos.title: Jeff Berlin …and Nothing But The Truth | Episode 19 Experimenting With Harmony on a 4-String Bass
videos.description:
Jeff Berlin …and Nothing But The Truth | Episode 19

In this episode, Jeff demonstrates how experimentation led him to deeper harmonic understanding on the bass.

Using the key of E as a foundation, he explores tensions, chord variations, and the process of discovering new musical ideas through private practice.

This isn’t a polished performance.
It’s real experimentation.

Sometimes learning harmony means bumping around, investigating, reviewing, and revisiting ideas until something clicks.

The bass may have limits — especially on four strings — but creativity does not.

Experiment.
Review.
Refine.

That’s how harmony develops.

Thank you for watching. Please like, subscribe, and turn on notifications for more episodes.

videos.title: 10,000 Hours… and Still Stuck?
videos.description:
The 10,000-hour rule sounds inspiring. Practice long enough and greatness follows.

But what if you’re practicing the wrong things?

If you spend years reviewing what you already know… chasing licks, warm-ups, hand tricks, and internet concepts… you may simply plateau more efficiently.

There are two paths:
1️⃣ Study academically written, harmonically centered music with a real teacher.
2️⃣ Teach yourself and accept the limits that come with it.

Ten years in, many players are still where they started. Not because they didn’t practice. Because they practiced without direction.

Hours don’t create growth. Correct information does.

Your thoughts are welcome.

#BassEducation #MusicPractice #10000Hours #JeffBerlin #MusicTheory #SeriousMusicians

videos.title: NAMM Predictability: Are We Teaching Obsolescence?
videos.description:
If you went to NAMM, you probably saw slappers, tappers, rock jams, blues jams, and lines for autographs. That’s the nature of a trade show. It sells what’s popular.

But here’s the real question:

Are we teaching music… or are we teaching trends?

From 1930s jazz to 70s disco to today’s contemporary bass techniques, styles always change. When you teach what’s current, you prepare for obsolescence. When you teach music itself, you prepare for a lifetime.

Before you invest years mastering a trend, consider what happens when the trend fades.

videos.title: Your Time Isn’t the Problem. Your Repertoire Is.
videos.description:
Many players believe that the more they work on their time, the better it will get. But time doesn’t improve in isolation.

Time is simply the ability to play something you’ve actually learned. When you understand a musical phrase, a line, or a song, playing it in time becomes natural.

Music is a language. You wouldn’t expect to speak a language with proper cadence before learning the words. The same is true here. Improving your bass playing comes from learning new music, expanding your repertoire, and engaging with new musical ideas. Time follows as a result.

Your time is probably fine.
Your musical vocabulary is where the real work lives.

Your thoughts are always welcome.

videos.title: Why Speeding Up the Metronome Is Slowing Your Progress
videos.description:
Most players think improving technique means slowly pushing the metronome faster and faster.
That approach confuses performance with learning.

Real improvement comes from slowing things down, even learning lines out of time, and spending more time understanding the music itself. Speed shows up later as a byproduct of comprehension, not pressure.

Practicing faster may give a temporary result on one line, but it doesn’t build real time feel or transferable musical ability.

Give this some thought.

videos.title: Why “Space Between the Notes” Isn’t Teachable
videos.description:
People often cite John Cage, Miles Davis, or Ron Carter when talking about “space between the notes.” But this idea breaks down fast.

You can’t practice it. You can’t define it. And you can’t teach it.

What can be taught and practiced are notes, harmony, rhythm, phrasing, articulation, and rests. Those players weren’t taught “space” when they were learning. They were taught fundamentals. The artistic interpretations came later.

For players trying to learn, mythologizing end-career concepts distracts from what actually builds musicianship.

Thoughts welcome.

videos.title: What Worked for Bass 40 Years Ago Still Works Today
videos.description:
A common claim today is that the way bass was taught 40 years ago “wouldn’t work now.” That idea doesn’t hold up.

Written music, harmony, rhythm, and learning how to execute them on the instrument worked then, it works now, and it will work decades from now. Classical training hasn’t changed for a reason. The fundamentals still produce results.

Modern bass education offers more options than ever, but variety has distracted many players from the most direct path to musical growth. If you give the fundamentals real time, not weeks, not months, but a year, the results are undeniable.

Thoughts welcome.

videos.title: Five Views Of A Secret
videos.description:
The bass world is full of well-worn ideas:
“Serve the song.”
“Space between the notes.”
“Play with soul, not notes.”
“Learn communication to get gigs.”
“Study Jaco forever.”

In this episode of Jeff Berlin …and Nothing But the Truth, Jeff Berlin challenges some of the most repeated principles in bass education and asks whether they actually help players improve or quietly hold them back.

Drawing from decades of experience and examples involving Miles Davis and Jaco Pastorius, Jeff argues that musicianship is built on notes, harmony, rhythm, phrasing, and disciplined study, not mythology or imitation.

This episode isn’t about tearing down heroes.
It’s about removing distractions and returning to what actually develops musicians.

Like, subscribe, and join the discussion.

videos.title: Why “Contemporary” Bass Education Is Failing Players | Jeff Berlin Driving Lesson
videos.description:
In this first Driving Lesson of 2026, Jeff Berlin explains why much of what is sold as “contemporary bass education” has failed to help players improve.

Bass education today has no shared standards, no checks and balances, and no agreed-upon musical truth. Instruction is shaped by trends, beliefs, and marketing language rather than by music itself. When schools or teachers promise “contemporary principles,” they are often selling ideas that change with time instead of teaching what remains constant in music: harmony, content, and musical understanding.

If you play a musical instrument, the requirement is music — not trends. The harmony found in nearly all contemporary songs, orchestral works, and small-group recordings has not changed. What has changed is how instruction is marketed.

This lesson challenges the idea that “new” teaching methods equal progress and asks players to consider where their time, money, and musical future are being invested.

Your thoughts are always welcome.
Happy New Year.

videos.title: Stop Practicing This | Jeff Berlin and Nothing But the Truth – Episode 13

Warming up doesn’t require special exercises. Playing the instrument itself does the job. Likewise, practicing music solves technical issues naturally, without adding unnecessary systems that don’t translate into performance.

This episode challenges popular habits and reinforces a simple truth:
music is learned by playing music.

If you’re serious about improving your bass playing, this episode will help you rethink what — and how — you practice.

videos.title: No One Cares If You Played It Open or Fretted
videos.description:
There’s a lot of internet talk about tone differences between open strings and fretted notes. Yes, the sonics can be different. But here’s the truth: no listener, no producer, and no gig will ever care which string you played the note on.

Producers listen to the group sound, not string choices. James Jamerson didn’t matter because he used open strings. He mattered because of who he was, how he learned, and how he played. Same with McCartney. The bass line is the bass line, not the position.

The internet is great for opinions. Just don’t confuse them with facts.

Play the notes where you want.
Nobody will be bothered.

Your thoughts are welcome.

#basslesson #drivinglessons #bassplayers #tone #musicianship #musictruth #recording #producers #musiceducation

videos.title: Walking Bass Lines: Why Perfection Beats GuessworkJeff Berlin and Nothing But the Truth | Episode 12
videos.description:
Walking bass lines have become increasingly popular, even among players who don’t play jazz. While walking bass is traditionally associated with jazz, the real value lies in what it teaches us about harmony, chord function, and knowing exactly where the notes are on the instrument.

In this episode of Jeff Berlin and Nothing But the Truth, Jeff explains why the common teaching approach of having students write their own walking bass lines and then “correcting” them is fundamentally flawed. Instead, Jeff argues that students benefit far more from practicing perfectly written walking bass lines from the very beginning, eliminating guesswork and reinforcing correct harmonic thinking.

Jeff demonstrates a functional walking bass line over a simple A7 blues, then shows how that same line can be developed with more harmonic depth and rhythmic feel. Along the way, he addresses a major misconception in bass education: that rhythm alone defines quality playing. True musical growth always combines rhythm and pitch, not one at the expense of the other.

If you’re serious about improving your bass playing, understanding harmony, and practicing with intention, this episode will challenge how you think about learning walking bass lines.

Don’t forget to like, subscribe, and turn on notifications for more episodes of Jeff Berlin and Nothing But the Truth.

#JeffBerlin #WalkingBass #BassEducation #JazzBass #BassLessons #MusicTheory #BassPlayers #NothingButTheTruth #WalkingBassLines

videos.title: Rhythm Alone Won’t Make You a Better Bass Player
videos.description:
Rhythm without pitch isn’t music.
And pitch without rhythm isn’t either.

Real bass playing has always required both harmony and time, together, not in sequence. Teaching rhythm first and “adding notes later” goes against the history of how music is actually learned.

If you’re self-taught, study players with great time. But in lessons, don’t accept the idea that rhythm and notes should be separated. They belong together.

Also… it’s freezing in Nashville.
If you’ve got a gig in Ecuador, call me.

Your thoughts are welcome.

#basslesson #rhythm #musictheory #bassplayers #musicianship #learningmusic #drivinglessons #practice #musiceducation

videos.title: Why You’re Not Improving… and How You Actually WILL
videos.description:

videos.title: The Truth: You Plateau Because You Ignore This One Thing
videos.description:
Here’s another Driving Lesson. Great musicians don’t improve by accident — they improve through daily practice. Some players go years without a day off, not because they’re obsessed, but because they understand that consistency is the engine of musical growth.

If you can give your bass 30 minutes a day, slowly and intentionally, you will improve. If you can give it an hour, even better. No click needed.

videos.title: Stop Chasing the Fret – Bass Playing Made Easier
videos.description: In this driving lesson, I debunk the common belief that you must press your finger right up against the fret to get the correct pitch on a fretted bass. The truth is, the note is defined by the string resting on the fret, not by how close your finger is to it. You can play anywhere between two frets and get the same pitch—often with less tension and more comfort. This simple adjustment can make your bass playing smoother and easier.

Is today’s bass education preparing students for musical obsolescence instead of longevity?

With over a century of music history in mind, Jeff dives deep into how styles come and go, but the language of music remains. Are students being trained to follow trends rather than becoming adaptable musicians? Is separating rhythm and pitch in bass teaching doing more harm than good?

✅ Like
✅ Subscribe
✅ Hit the bell for future episodes featuring demos, interviews, and real talk about the future of bass.

Join the conversation in the comments—Jeff wants to hear your thoughts.
Music is changing. Are you ready?

videos.title: Jeff Berlin ...and nothing but the truth - Episode #3
videos.description:
Jeff Berlin – ...and nothing but the truth | Episode 3
Nobody dances to a note? Jeff responds.

In this episode, Jeff Berlin dives into a widespread belief in the music world — that notes don’t make people move. With his direct approach, Jeff explains why that idea falls short and how focusing too little on pitch can hold musicians back.

What’s inside:
– The importance of notes in feel and groove
– Why pitch still matters for rhythm players
– A demo using three metronome tempos — with no notes
– Thoughts on musical fluency and long-term growth

If you’re serious about music, this one’s for you.

Leave a comment if you’ve heard (or believed) that groove doesn’t come from notes.
